title: A land emerges from ice
content: At the end of the last Ice Age, huge ice sheets covered what is now Norway. As the climate warmed, ice retreated north and carved fjords, valleys, and coastlines. This harsh but resource-rich landscape shaped everything that followed.
next: Who were the first people to move into this new environment?

title: First settlers follow the ice edge
content: Early hunter-gatherers entered newly ice-free zones while following animal migrations. They used rivers and coasts as movement corridors and food sources. Archaeological traces show seasonal camps, stone tools, and fishing traditions.
next: Why did coastal life become so central in Norway?

title: Farming arrives but never fully replaces fishing
content: Agriculture spread into southern Norway, including barley, wheat, and livestock. Yet short growing seasons and rugged terrain limited crop farming. Many communities stayed mixed economies: part farming, part fishing, part hunting.
next: How did trade pull Norway into wider Bronze Age networks?

title: Bronze Age links and ship skill
content: Norway lacked copper and tin, so bronze had to be imported through trade. This encouraged long-distance exchange and improved seafaring. Rock carvings show boats and crews, hinting that maritime identity was already deepening.
next: What changed in society during the Iron Age?

title: Chieftains, oral culture, and social layers
content: Local chieftains led territories, while free farmers held rights and slaves occupied the lowest status. Norse religion, heroic poetry, and runic writing reinforced shared culture. Power was personal and local, not yet a stable kingdom.
next: What technology turned this society outward?

title: The longship revolution
content: Norwegian shipbuilders perfected longships: fast, shallow-draft vessels that crossed open seas and entered rivers. This made surprise attacks, long-range trade, and migration possible at scale. Mobility became Norway's strategic advantage.
next: Why did raids begin in the late 700s?

title: Lindisfarne shocks Europe
content: The attack on Lindisfarne monastery became a symbolic start of the Viking Age in Western memory. Coastal religious centers were wealthy and lightly defended. Raiding spread fear but also opened routes that became trade and settlement channels.
next: Were Vikings only raiders, or something more?

title: Raiders, traders, settlers
content: Norwegians founded bases and settlements in places like Dublin, Iceland, and Greenland. They traded furs, fish, and ivory while also trafficking enslaved people. Viking expansion combined violence, commerce, adaptation, and state-building pressure at home.
next: How did conflict inside Norway lead to unification?

title: Harald Fairhair and the unification idea
content: After decades of fighting rival rulers, Harald Fairhair is traditionally linked to victory at Hafrsfjord. His rule did not create instant stability, but it created something new: the idea of Norway as one kingdom under one ruler.
next: What happened when many chieftains rejected this new order?

title: Exile and Atlantic expansion
content: Some opponents of royal centralization left rather than submit. Large migration to Iceland and other islands expanded the Norwegian world overseas. Domestic power struggles and Atlantic colonization were therefore linked, not separate stories.
next: How did the Viking political world end in 1066?

title: The Viking Age closes
content: Harald Hardrada's death at Stamford Bridge marked the end of the era of large Scandinavian conquest attempts in England. Norway was moving toward a Christian, more bureaucratic monarchy. The age of raiding gave way to medieval kingdom politics.
next: Next: Christianity, kingship, and medieval Norway.
